original_hypothesis.title: Permanent Lock of Bridged Assets via Push-over-Pull ETH Refund DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The Bridge and Vault contracts use a push-over-pull pattern for ETH refunds and asset recalls. If the refund recipient (refundTo) or source owner is a smart contract that reverts on receiving ETH, the entire transaction reverts, permanently locking the bridged assets in the NEW state.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Pushing ETH to arbitrary addresses is a known anti-pattern. Since processMessage reverts entirely if the refund fails, the message can never reach the FAILED state, preventing the user from recalling their assets on the source chain. This represents a permanent loss of funds for users bridging to smart contract wallets (like Gnosis Safe) that lack a payable fallback.

proof_of_signal.pattern: refundTo.sendEther(_message.fee + refundAmount) pushes ETH and reverts on failure

preconditions: User bridges assets to a destination address or specifies a refundTo address that is a smart contract
The target contract reverts on receiving ETH (e.g., missing receive function or out of gas)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: refundTo.sendEther() reverts the entire processMessage transaction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Message status remains NEW forever because processMessage cannot complete
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but results in 100% loss of bridged assets for the victim due to permanent lock

economic_questions: Can a user intentionally grief themselves to lock funds?
Are there any fallback mechanisms to recover stuck NEW messages?
validation_requirements: Test processMessage with a reverting refundTo contract
Verify that recallMessage cannot be called for messages stuck in NEW on the destination chain

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: High impact on users of smart contract wallets

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users bridging to smart contract wallets
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of bridged assets in the specific transaction
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets bridged to incompatible contracts

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check sendEther gas limits and fallback behavior
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Validate state machine transitions when processMessage reverts

validation_result.impact_summary: If refundTo or destOwner is a smart contract that reverts on receiving ETH (or on zero-value calls), processMessage permanently reverts, locking the message in NEW state. The failure signal is never emitted, preventing recallMessage on the source chain. Bridged assets become permanently unrecoverable.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Bridge.processMessage function performs ETH refunds via LibAddress.sendEther, which uses ExcessivelySafeCall and reverts with ETH_TRANSFER_FAILED if the call returns false. If the refundTo address (derived from _message.refundTo or defaulting to _message.destOwner) is a contract without a receive/fallback function, or one that reverts on ETH receipt, the entire processMessage transaction reverts. Since status transitions and proof receipt deletions are all rolled back, the message remains permanently in NEW state. The FAILED status (required for recallMessage on the source chain) is only set via retryMessage after RETRIABLE, but RETRIABLE requires processMessage to succeed first. Even when refundAmount is 0 and fee is 0, sendEther(addr, 0) is still called, which executes the target contract's code and can revert. This creates an unrecoverable deadlock for bridged assets.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in Bridge.sol processMessage where sendEther is called unconditionally after status updates, and in LibAddress.sol where sendEther reverts on any failure including 0-value transfers to reverting contracts. There is no try/catch, no pull pattern fallback, and no short-circuit for zero amounts.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: sendEther reverts the entire processMessage if refundTo is a contract that cannot receive ETH. The status transition (DONE/RETRIABLE) written earlier in the function is rolled back, permanently trapping the message in NEW state.

No short-circuit for _amount == 0. Even zero-value calls to contracts without receive/fallback will return success=false and revert. The function always reverts on failure with no graceful fallback mechanism.

calling_sequence: 1. processMessage validates message hash and status == NEW
4. _invokeMessageCall executes the message (or special address handling)
5. _updateMessageStatus sets status to DONE or RETRIABLE
8. If refundTo contract reverts -> sendEther reverts -> processMessage reverts
9. ALL state changes (steps 3-5) are rolled back, message stays NEW
10. Message can never reach FAILED state, recallMessage impossible on source chain
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: processMessage -> _updateMessageStatus(DONE/RETRIABLE) -> refundTo.sendEther() -> LibAddress.sendEther() -> ExcessivelySafeCall.excessivelySafeCall() returns false -> revert ETH_TRANSFER_FAILED(). The revert rolls back all state changes including the status transition, leaving the message permanently in NEW. On the source chain, recallMessage requires a FAILED signal from the destination, which is never generated because: (a) FAILED is only set in retryMessage, (b) retryMessage requires RETRIABLE status, (c) RETRIABLE is only set in processMessage which permanently reverts.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts affect this vulnerability. The defect exists in the core Bridge.sol and LibAddress.sol code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: 1. User calls ERC20Vault.sendToken on source chain, setting refundTo to a contract address that reverts on ETH receipt (or the user is interacting through a contract wallet without receive/fallback). The message is sent with some fee > 0. 2. On the destination chain, any relayer calls processMessage. The message invocation may succeed (tokens delivered), but when the bridge attempts to refund the remaining fee to refundTo via sendEther, the call fails. 3. The entire processMessage transaction reverts. The message status remains NEW. 4. Every subsequent attempt to call processMessage for this message will also fail identically. 5. On the source chain, the user cannot call recallMessage because the message has not FAILED on the destination chain (it's stuck in NEW). 6. The user's tokens remain locked in the source vault permanently. Note: even if fee=0 and value=0, the sendEther(refundTo, 0) call still executes the contract code and can revert.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Users who bridge assets through smart contract wallets or specify refundTo as contracts that cannot receive ETH (or revert on empty calls) will permanently lose their bridged assets. The tokens are locked in the source vault with no recovery mechanism. While most popular smart contract wallets (like Gnosis Safe) typically have fallback functions, custom contracts, some proxy implementations, and certain DeFi protocol contracts may lack them. The vulnerability also affects onMessageRecalled in all vault contracts where srcOwner.sendEther is used.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a pull pattern for ETH refunds: store pending refunds in a mapping and allow recipients to claim them later
Add a short-circuit in sendEther to skip the call when _amount == 0: if (_amount == 0) return;
Wrap the sendEther calls in a try/catch-like pattern: if the refund fails, store the amount in a claimable mapping instead of reverting
Move the refund logic before the state transition, or separate state commitment from refund distribution
Consider using a two-step approach: update message status first in a separate transaction, then allow claiming refunds
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bridge and ERC20Vault on two test chains. 2. Deploy a contract without receive/fallback on destination chain. 3. Send a bridge message with refundTo set to the reverting contract, fee > 0, and some ERC20 tokens. 4. On destination chain, call processMessage - observe it reverts with ETH_TRANSFER_FAILED. 5. Verify message status remains NEW. 6. Attempt recallMessage on source chain - verify it fails because no FAILED signal exists. 7. Verify tokens are permanently locked in source vault.
uncertainties: The exact prevalence of smart contract wallets/addresses that revert on 0-value empty calls is uncertain - most popular wallets have fallback functions
Whether the Solidity compiler-generated code for contracts without receive/fallback reverts on zero-value calls at the EVM level needs bytecode-level verification for specific contract versions
additional_testing_needed: Test with various smart contract wallet implementations (Gnosis Safe, Argent, etc.) to determine which ones trigger the DoS
Test with 0 value and 0 fee to confirm whether sendEther(addr, 0) still fails for contracts without receive
Test the complete message lifecycle to confirm the message is truly unrecoverable once stuck in NEW state
Fuzz test with various contract types as refundTo to identify the full attack surface
